content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You bought a display. A slab of glass whose entire job is to receive pixels and show them. Then you plugged it in, rebooted, and a McAfee ad greeted you like an uninvited houseguest who also brought a friend.
This is not a hypothetical. It is happening right now to owners of LG monitors, and the delivery mechanism is Windows itself.
What actually happened
In July 2026, a Reddit user on r/pcmasterrace connected LG UltraGear monitors (a 27GP83B and two 27GN800 units) and later found an app called LG Monitor App Installer on the system. Nobody downloaded it. Nobody clicked anything. Windows Reliability Monitor showed the package 9PM9N6F47JB8-LGElectronics.LGMonitorApp arriving quietly through the Microsoft Store as part of a background update.
The app’s first visible act was not calibrating colors or updating firmware. It was a popup offering a free 30 day trial of McAfee Scam Detector. The irony of an unsolicited app warning you about scams writes itself.
Gamers Nexus then reproduced the whole thing with a $1,200 LG UltraGear 34GX900A-B. Fresh Windows install, plug in the monitor, and Windows Update silently pulled LG’s packages with no consent prompt. They rebooted the machine 32 times. The McAfee promotion appeared on 31 of those boots. On the one remaining boot, the app took…
